full_transcript.text: What's the date on this one?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: April 29, 2024. So this is—

full_transcript.text: No, it's not a top-down thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis

full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Take a look, see if you can find that for me.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They are going to be fighting themselves. Their unions and everything are going to be fighting and there's going to be a whole thing. So they—they know that this is how—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So Uber, this isn't about Uber versus Lyft. This isn't about DoorDash versus Waymo doing deliveries or robotaxis or Zoox. This is about a civil war inside of these companies. So it's 1% this year, next year it's 2, year after it's 4, year after it's 8, year after it's 16. You want to give them some hope that they can keep their job for some amount of time.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And that's what all this is. Now people are going to say, 'Oh, it's hypocritical.' It seems hypocritical from the outside, yes. full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but it's—there's a gulf between now and then that you gotta get through if you're DoorDash.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So managing the transition is what's occurring here. So everybody's going to dunk on them and say, 'Oh, they're trying to be decels.' full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: That's it, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I mean, I think it's—there's so many things to look out for. I mean, if you're DoorDash next year, you still need humans. It's—it's fascinating you take that tack because there was also—I don't know if you saw this letter today—the economists published about AI job displacement.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I did not. And this is after everybody is suddenly swung to the other side of the ship and is like—everyone's like, 'Oh, no, there's going to be no AI displacement.' I'm like, guys, the ship is like, whoop! Everybody's opinion was massive job destruction, now it's no job displacement.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So yes, so 200 economists including 15 Nobel laureates published an open letter today. It's called 'We Must Act Now.' Warns that AI is going to radically transform the U.S. economy and that we have to have a plan to offset potentially millions of lost white-collar jobs. It was signed by the chief economist for both OpenAI and Anthropic. Also Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, and venture capitalist Vinod Khosla, friend of the show. Uh, so—but I think the point they're making here, Jason, is almost exactly the one that you and I were just talking about. It's going to uptake productivity, we're going to find new jobs and new sectors we don't even think about today, and—and we'll be fine. But they're saying in the—in the gulf between...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: between today and that future, it's going to be a huge problem. And the transition is what's going to be so rocky and that we need to manage. full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So do you think Uber should—do you think Uber and DoorDash should slow down through legislation on a per-state basis the deployment of self-driving in order to save jobs and ostensibly not have accidents and try to build these hybrid networks? full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I mean, I don't think companies should worry about being hypocritical personally. Like I think they should just worry about, you know, what the most important thing is in the immediate future for themselves, their own health and the people that work for them. I definitely think that we should be thoughtful. And if that means pumping the brakes a little bit, obviously I don't think we should not pursue the future where automation is happening. I think that's coming, it's inevitable. But I'm—I'm not against ideas like this of like, let's slow down, let's make sure that we still have a functional economy next year as we wait for 20 years from now.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Would you be agree with licensing and say New Jersey, if New Jersey has—I'm just going to make up a number here—250,000 DoorDashers, Lyft and Uber drivers, 250 drivers, we're going to release 1% of cars per year, 2% next year, 4% next year. Would you be okay with that? And then they charge them $5,000 a year, $10,000 a year for that license and that money goes towards, you know, like maybe unemployment for those drivers or something. Would you be okay with something like that?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yes. In fact, I like those kinds of systems. I think we were talking about another thing, there was a proposal for like we should put a tax on companies as they replace people with AI to offset, you know, like every time you replace another human job with an AI, that's all right, but you put like an extra 5% in the—in the tank for other people who are getting laid off. Like I like ideas like that. I think managed capitalism, I don't have an ideological problem with that at all. I think we should do more of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: By the way, we already have that in most states. You have to put money away as an employer into your unemployment fund.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: And then, yeah, people take that. I don't know exactly how—

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So this would just be like the AI version of the unemployment fund. But I think I'm not—like, I, you know, I used to be—when in college, I was like a socialist, full-on communist. Like I think I've adapted to where it's like, you can have capitalism, you just regulate it carefully to make sure it's not abusive to people. I don't—I don't have an ideological problem with that at all. I think we should do more of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I mean, listen, if everybody wants to know where I stand, I think we should have licensing and safety is the number one thing. The great paradox here is even if this gets slowed down and let's say people are right, Uber and Lyft and DoorDash are going to slow this down in order to catch up with their products, like that's kind of the insinuation. They don't want to ban it, they just want to slow it down and they want to—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: manage their own employees during the transition. Okay, let's say both of those things are true.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: And they might turn out to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I still think I would want to see, I still think the rollout is not coming that fast. I still think it's going to be 1%, 2%, 4%, 8%, 16%. So let's just do that number. And I think right now, ride-sharing is 1% of all rides that occur in the United States.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And then I think of that 1%, so take now we have a 100% pie of rides, I think that like with the exception of like top markets, Los Angeles and San Francisco, of ride-sharing rides, maybe less than 1% are automated now.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: It might be two.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So if we assume 2% a year, 3% a year, okay, 2027 it goes to 4%. 2028 it goes to 8%. 2029 it goes to 16%. 2030 we hit our stride, it goes to 30%.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's still going to take time, right? And I think all these proposals are two-year proposals or three-year proposals. So I think the organic rollout as Waymo and Zoox and Tesla will want to do it is going to go relatively at the same pace as these proposals that are the transition.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: That makes sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So it's going to be moot at the end. It's just people lobbying for something that's going to be a paced rollout anyway. It's going to take five years to get to 30% automated.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right. Your numbers were really close. 1 to 2% of all rides in the US currently are ride shares and 0.2 to 5%.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: This is according to Gemini. 0.2 to 0.5% of those ride shares are in AVs currently.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Got it. Okay, so let's say it goes from 1 in 200 this year.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And that's max, it's 0.2 to 0.5%. So 1 in 200, we'll give it the benefit of the doubt.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Exactly, exactly. 1 in 200 this year, 1 in 100 next. I'm saying doubling. So 1 in 100 next year, 1 in 50 in 2028, 1 in 25 '29, 1 in 12 in 2030, 1 in 6.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And that's if it's perfectly consistent.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: In '31, 1 in 3 in '32. So maybe we have half in another seven years. I think that's actually kind of realistic. I think we may be sitting here at this clip and be like, JCal nailed it.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Perfect, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Again. I mean, I think it's really like it jumps when it comes into a big new market because I think there are a lot of people like myself, the very first time you take a Waymo ride, you're like, well, this is fantastic. I'm going to do this as often as I can.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Like you see it, it sells a lot of people after one ride, I think.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's really a safety issue, and that's actually what I'm very nervous about. Having seen this movie before, I think there is a very significant chance that all of this gets paused if just two people get killed in.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, two people, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Man, yeah, the video, if there's a graphic video out there, yeah, that's a problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, no, two people die in a quarter or one person dies on video because of a mistake or gets hospitalized.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It is on video.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: It's by default it is on video because these all have ten cameras.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, I mean if it gets out there in the world and we are all on social media watching a graphic video of a Waymo killing somebody, I think that's how that would happen.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: When there's a lawsuit, there'll be a FOIA, there'll be a freedom of information when there's a lawsuit, and a pixelated version of it or a version right before the person gets hit will be released.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: So it's either going to be, you know, some grocery store's camera, speaking of Flock, some grocery store camera, a Flock camera, whatever, somebody's going to pick it up and we're going to have it. There's cameras everywhere, so, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I am going to make two predictions. 50% of rides are automated 2032. Okay? 2032, 2033, okay? So six years from now.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I'm going to say seven years.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, so seven years to 50%. So all of this hand-wringing over this, oh my god, Uber's de-selling, DoorDash and Lyft are going to de-sell. It's going to be moot because they're all proposing three-year, two or three-year transitionary periods, so, yeah, it's going to be moot.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Second thing, I think there'll be a one-year pause in all of this, where I think it's a greater than 50% chance that we might hit like a one or two-year pause during that seven years when something very bad happens.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I don't want to see that happen, but...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: There's so much pressure on these companies, Lon. Like if you look at the Tesla bulls, they're all these crazy Tesla accounts that are like, they've placed a bet on Tesla stock and then they release five videos a day pushing Tesla to go faster and faster and like they're like, 'I've never gotten an accident with FSD' and it's like, 'Of course you haven't, like you're doing a ride a day'.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: And oh, the Waymo hasn't had an accident.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Except they hit a kid in Santa Monica at seven miles an hour and like that could have been a lot worse.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and I mean, I think both sides, like you can see their point. Like statistically, these cars are so much safer than human drivers. Like if you were doing any kind of test of human drivers across US cities, like people hitting each other and they hit things and they hit other cars all the time, every day.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: People die all the time, 30,000 a year.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Every single day. So, yeah, it's undeniable just if you're looking at it as raw data, but the emotional impact of seeing an automated car hit a person invalidates all of that for people. The moment they see that, exactly, it becomes emotional, it stops being about the numbers and it's like, 'Oh my god, that robot car murdered a human, we have to stop this.' And I think that that's the mindset for a lot of people.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: That's exactly the mindset.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay. Great.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: We got what... You want to talk about Flock real quick before we...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Well, you saw my tweet, yeah, so I'm happy to talk about it a little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So Flock Safety CEO Garrett Langley did an interview where he called De-Flock a terrorist organization. De-Flock, of course, an open-source map of license plate cameras in the US. Anybody can report new cameras that they spot. Justine Bateman, the, the actress and filmmaker responded, she's criticized Flock's claims that they're not forcing Flock on anyone.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: You responded to Ms. Bateman, claiming that Flock has a PR problem. We can bring up the tweet here. "Flock is doing good work but their comms are sub-optimal at best. I've seen this movie before with Robinhood and Uber and it's an easy fix. CEO founder owns the issue and walks people through it step by step over and over again until there's no way for the issue to be debated in bad faith." So you want to unpack this for us a little bit?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I think they took notes from Trump and said we have to go on the offensive, we have to go on the attack and say hey, these people who are burning down or taking down the Flock cameras and vandalizing them, they're terrorists. Okay, they're not terrorists. You know, like that's... we all know what a terrorist is. So when you use that hyperbolic language and at the same time people have valid concerns, then you are going to activate people.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And that's what they've inadvertently done. It would be like Travis coming out and there's surge pricing and he says "Deal with it. Stop being poor." Right? If he were to say something that outlandish or that hyperbolic, it's going to make people a concern. So here's what you have to do. You do what Travis did. We worked on a blog post together back in the day about surge pricing. Because when he would explain surge pricing to me, it made total sense and I was totally for it. When the public talked about it, when he wasn't talking about it, they would be like "Oh my God, they're charging, you know, six times the normal price to take me and my child to the doctor." And it's like "During a blizzard?" You're taking your child to a doctor during the blizzard?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Literally that's what it was. It was a rich person, I can't remember which reality TV star was like "They just tried to charge me six times as much to pick up my kid during the blizzard" and like "this is abusive." And it was like, okay, there's no car service to pick up your kid either. Everybody just shuts down. Uber happened to build a system that says we're going to double the price until somebody takes the ride. Would you like to have Uber never give an incentive for drivers to come out, which means they're not available on New Year's, they're not available during a snowstorm or rain, they just stay home because why risk it as a driver, or would you like to just have availability? And so the argument was not like price gouging, it was availability. But it took us a while to get there. Here, what Flock is saying is like, hey, don't pay attention to what we're doing. We have no culpability. We provide software to local jurisdictions and there's a bunch of terrorists and they're attacking us and we're hurt. The reason people are attacking you is because there are a bunch of people who really value their privacy.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Now, should they attack you? Absolutely not. But, you know, taking a bat or a paint gun or a shotgun to a, you know, a speed camera as an example or a license plate reading camera, that's like a time-honored tradition at this point. Like people hate being surveilled.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's also very European, in Europe they shot, or not shot, they...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They vandalized all the cameras on the highways there, speed cameras specifically. People get the ick from this. So what you have to do as the founder is to explain it from first principles.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And when I had him on the show, the founder, I was really walking him through this actually because I identified it back then. And what they said was, every time I asked him like, "What's the data retention policy? How do you track who has access to this stuff? Like, if a rogue cop wants to track their, you know, their divorce and they want to track their spouse, can they abuse the system, right?"

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And they were like, "Well, the local community sets their things," or whatever. And it was a much better answer to say, "We only allow a local police officer to use this software if they keep the license plates only for up to 12 months. They're not allowed to keep them past 12 months."

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Additionally, any time they track a license plate, they have to put their badge number in, they have to put their password in, and it tells us in a permanent log that we keep at our headquarters, not the police, that says Officer Crumpsky looked at Mrs. Smith's license plate, and he looked at it again on this day.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And if we see, and then all of that goes in an audit to the city council, and we only allow this product to be installed in places where there is a city council and a judge and an organization that looks out for this type of abuse.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We do not allow facial recognition, we do not take pictures of that, and all data's gone in 12 months. So if there's a series of robberies and it goes on for two years, the police can only get one year of data. And if it gets leaked, you know, we know it's going to get leaked.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Now, if you said it the way I said it, and we only install when the community is 75% in favor of this. So if there's a community that is 75% in favor of it, we allow them to put this technology in. If 75% is against it, we don't want them to have the technology and you can't have it there because the police can't unilaterally put it in. They need to have community support.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And all of the locations are publicly available, and we're available if there's an audit. Any judge can come to us, we will give them the logs of potential abuse.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The end.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, I feel like they are, like your approach definitely comes from a place of, "I'm aware that there are very justified concerns about this, so we're going to address them."

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: The end.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, I feel like Flock keeps coming in from this perspective like, "Why are you criticizing us? It doesn't make any sense." Like, like they had that Super Bowl ad that was like, "We're going to use your doorbell cam to help spy on your neighbors and find your lost dog," and then they were...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That was Ring.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Well, it was the Flock—it was Flock, they were collaborating with Ring on this, and then Ring cut off the collaboration with Flock after the negative backlash to that ad.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And then I just feel like every time, like there's a...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: There's that... the refrain seems to be, 'Well, if you hate safety... like, would you rather have a safe neighborhood or your privacy?'

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Are you pro-criminal? Yeah. You're either pro-criminal anti-Flock, or you're anti-criminal and pro-Flock.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And I feel like you just lose people right away with that. There's no nuance. It's not a binary. These are justifiable concerns and you just... people are going to shut down if that's your response, I think.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: Exactly. And, you know, the way to say that is, hey, by the way... with AI, we know it's a dog. When it's a lost dog, if people in Flock want to add the lost children/lost dog feature, they can opt into it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes. Right. I think it makes total sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Tibo Louis
full_transcript.text: If you don't want to have your Ring camera as part of this, and you have privacy concerns, you don't have to. You can just record your property. highlights: Building in public serves as a powerful validation tool; shipping new projects weekly on social media can compound a founder's influence and provide critical feedback, even if early attempts are unsuccessful.

To scale a startup via social media, founders should partner with micro-influencers using low base fees combined with high performance bonuses to capitalize on algorithm-driven virality rather than static follower counts.

The legal friction between Apple and OpenAI highlights the strategic risk of "incinerating" high-value partnerships by aggressively poaching talent and entering a partner's core business, such as consumer hardware.

While autonomous vehicle adoption is predicted to reach 50% of all rides by 2032, the industry will likely face significant regulatory pauses and public backlash following inevitable graphic accidents captured on social media.

Companies in sensitive sectors like surveillance must prioritize radical transparency, such as using public audit logs and community opt-in requirements, to mitigate PR crises and address valid privacy concerns.
summary:
In this episode of *This Week in Startups*, Jason Calacanis and Lon Harris explore the evolving landscape of startup growth, corporate strategy, and the societal impacts of automation. Guest Tibo Louis-Lucas shares his "build in public" philosophy, explaining how shipping multiple projects and iterating based on community feedback helped him achieve rapid success with products like Tweet Hunter. A key tactical focus is the shift in influencer marketing; Tibo advocates for partnering with micro-influencers through performance-based revenue shares rather than high flat fees, leveraging social media algorithms to achieve viral growth without the need for a massive personal following.

The discussion pivots to high-stakes industry conflicts, specifically Apple’s lawsuit against OpenAI for allegedly poaching hardware talent and stealing trade secrets. Calacanis critiques Sam Altman’s aggressive expansion, arguing that the short-term gain of hiring Apple executives may have cost OpenAI a multi-trillion-dollar partnership. This theme of managed friction continues with a debate over autonomous vehicle (AV) legislation in D.C. and China. The hosts discuss the "civil war" within companies like Uber and DoorDash, which must balance the inevitable transition to robotics with the immediate need to support and manage millions of human drivers. Calacanis predicts that while 50% of rides will be automated by 2032, the industry will likely face significant regulatory pauses following high-profile safety incidents.

Finally, the episode addresses the growing tension between public safety and digital privacy through the lens of Flock Safety’s surveillance technology. Calacanis argues that the company’s combative PR approach has backfired, suggesting that transparency, strict audit logs, and community consent are essential for navigating the "ick factor" of a surveillance state. The show concludes with a tribute to the late actor Sam Neill and a look at the emerging subculture of AI-enhanced fan edits in cinema, illustrating the broader theme of how technology is empowering individuals to reshape existing industries and media.

Today’s show:

Jason spent $11 building out PodMeme, an AI tool that tracks down the best segments from top podcasts about a specific topic, then stitches them together into a single stream. His post laying out how he did it, featuring a simple screenshot of the product, reached 1.1M views in just a few days.

It’s a simple fact: builders love to see what other people are building, and growing your new project “in public,” sharing your journey on social media, is a great way to develop buzz around your product before it even exists.

Today’s guest, Thibault “Tibo” Louis-Lucas, walks us through how he grew products like Tweet Hunter and Taplio on his social media account, using social media buzz and a focus on tools for creators to very quickly expand to over $1M ARR.

PLUS why Apple would only sue OpenAI if they knew they had them dead to rights… why Uber and Waymo are fighting over DC robotaxis… Jason’s response to the public backlash against surveillance startup Flock Safety… and the guys remember iconic New Zealand actor Sam Neill.
